narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your background, like choosing full-time study and coming from a middle-income family. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 51.6% chance of failure for you, which tipped the decision to reject your application to help ensure students are set up for long-term success. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic rankings in your early undergraduate years—your Year 1 decile of 1 was the biggest factor pushing the model toward expecting a bar exam struggle, as it's well below the typical student's standing around the middle of their class. Your Year 3 decile of 2 also played a significant role in that direction, reinforcing concerns despite some later improvement. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 33.5 stood out positively, pulling the prediction toward success more than any other favorable factor, as it's closer to the average of 36.7 seen in students who typically pass. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 helped counterbalance some risks, outperforming expectations in nudging toward passing compared to the dataset average of 3.21. Your middle-income family background and full-time enrollment status provided smaller but supportive influences toward a positive outcome. Compared to typical students in the dataset, your early deciles are notably lower, while your LSAT and GPA are reasonably competitive, which is why those early grades carried the most weight in this close call. We know this rejection may feel disheartening, especially given the unique challenges many Black women face in competitive academic paths, but it's not a reflection of your potential—it's just one model's snapshot based on patterns from past bar takers. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted improvement in your coursework, perhaps by seeking tutoring or study groups tailored to law school prep. Retaking the LSAT to push closer to or above the average could dramatically shift these predictions in your favor. You're capable of rewriting this story—keep pursuing your goals with the resilience that's gotten you this far, and consider reapplying after addressing these key areas. We're rooting for you.
